Description: Configurable constructors using variadic function arguments that modify a config struct
When To Use: When a struct has many optional parameters; when you want clean API for configuration
When Not To Use: When only 1-2 simple parameters; when configuration is always required
Anti-Pattern: func NewServer(port int, timeout time.Duration, maxConn int, tls bool, logger *log.Logger) *Server { ... } // Too many positional params

Description: Constructor function that returns a new instance with validation and default values
When To Use: When you need validation at creation time; when zero value is not useful
When Not To Use: When zero value of the struct is already useful
Anti-Pattern: u := User{} // Skipping validation, zero value may be invalid

Description: Thread-safe lazy initialization of a single instance using sync.Once
When To Use: When exactly one instance must exist (DB pool, logger); when initialization is expensive
When Not To Use: When testing requires different instances; when you need multiple configurations
Anti-Pattern: var db = connectDB() // Eager init, fails if DB unavailable at startup\n// Or using mutex: mu.Lock(); if db == nil { db = connect() }; mu.Unlock()

Description: Construct complex objects step by step using method chaining
When To Use: When object construction has many steps; when you want readable construction code
When Not To Use: When simple constructor or functional options suffice
Anti-Pattern: Constructing complex SQL as raw string concatenation

Description: Factory function that enforces invariants at creation time
When To Use: When struct has required fields or constraints; when invalid state should be impossible
When Not To Use: When all fields are optional or zero value is valid
Anti-Pattern: type EmailAddress string // No validation, anyone can create invalid email

Description: Wrapping http.Handler to add cross-cutting concerns like logging, auth, CORS
When To Use: For cross-cutting concerns in HTTP servers; for adding behavior without modifying handlers
When Not To Use: When behavior is specific to a single handler; when it adds too much complexity
Anti-Pattern: Putting logging/auth logic directly inside every handler function

Description: Convert one interface to another to make incompatible types work together
When To Use: When integrating third-party code with your interfaces; when wrapping legacy APIs
When Not To Use: When types already satisfy the required interface
Anti-Pattern: Modifying the legacy code directly to match your interface

Description: Provide a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
When To Use: When a subsystem has many components that are used together; when simplifying API for consumers
When Not To Use: When the subsystem is already simple; when callers need fine-grained control
Anti-Pattern: Requiring callers to coordinate inventory, payment, and shipping themselves

Description: Compose types by embedding structs/interfaces to promote methods
When To Use: When you want to reuse behavior without inheritance; when extending types with additional methods
When Not To Use: When you need to hide the embedded type's methods; when it creates confusing method sets

Description: Keep interfaces small and focused on a single responsibility
When To Use: Always in Go — prefer 1-3 method interfaces; when consumers only need part of functionality
When Not To Use: Rarely — Go idiom strongly favors small interfaces
Anti-Pattern:
type FileManager interface {
    Read(p []byte) (int, error)
    Write(p []byte) (int, error)
    Close() error
    Seek(offset int64, whence int) (int64, error)
    Stat() (os.FileInfo, error)
} // Too many methods

Description: Define a family of algorithms as interfaces and swap them at runtime
When To Use: When behavior needs to be swappable (sorting, compression, auth strategies)
When Not To Use: When there is only one implementation and no need for flexibility
Anti-Pattern: Hardcoding algorithm choice with if/else or switch in business logic

Description: Use channels to implement event notification pattern
When To Use: When multiple goroutines need to react to events; for decoupled event-driven communication
When Not To Use: When a simple function callback suffices; when there is only one observer
Anti-Pattern: Using global callback functions that create tight coupling

Description: Encapsulate a request as an object to parameterize clients with operations
When To Use: When you need undo/redo; when queuing or logging operations; for transaction scripts
When Not To Use: When operations are simple and don't need encapsulation
Anti-Pattern: Putting all operations in a single large function with no ability to undo

Description: Connect processing stages via channels where each stage is a goroutine
When To Use: For stream processing; when data flows through multiple transformation steps
When Not To Use: When processing is simple and sequential; when overhead of goroutines is not justified
Anti-Pattern: Processing all stages synchronously in a single loop when data is streamed

Description: Pass request along a chain of handlers until one handles it
When To Use: For middleware chains; for request validation pipelines; for fallback handling
When Not To Use: When only one handler is needed; when order doesn't matter
Anti-Pattern: Nesting if/else for every check in a single function

Description: Use maps or slices of structs to drive behavior instead of complex conditionals
When To Use: For tests (table-driven tests); for routing; for configuration-driven logic
When Not To Use: When there are only 2-3 simple cases

Description: Functions should accept interface parameters and return concrete types
When To Use: Always in library code; when you want flexibility for callers
When Not To Use: When the concrete type is needed for specific functionality
Anti-Pattern: func NewCacheService(store *RedisStore) *CacheService { ... } // Locks to specific impl

Description: Add context to errors using fmt.Errorf with %w verb
When To Use: Always when propagating errors upward; when caller needs error chain
When Not To Use: When you want to hide the original error from callers
Anti-Pattern: if err != nil { return err } // No context added\n// or: return fmt.Errorf("failed: %v", err) // %v loses error chain

Description: Pass context.Context as the first parameter through all layers
When To Use: Always in concurrent code; in HTTP handlers; in database queries; in gRPC calls
When Not To Use: In pure computation functions with no I/O or cancellation need
Anti-Pattern: func (s *Service) GetUser(id int) (*User, error) { ... } // No context = no cancellation

Description: Use type switch for handling multiple concrete types from an interface
When To Use: When processing different types from an interface value; for error type handling
When Not To Use: When you can use method dispatch (polymorphism) instead
Anti-Pattern: if s, ok := val.(string); ok { ... } else if i, ok := val.(int); ok { ... } // Nested if/else

Description: Implement fmt.Stringer interface for human-readable type representation
When To Use: When your type will be printed or logged; for enum-like types with iota
When Not To Use: When the default struct printing is sufficient
Anti-Pattern: fmt.Sprintf("day=%d", day) // Printing raw int value everywhere

Description: Compose functionality by chaining io.Reader and io.Writer implementations
When To Use: When processing streams of data; for building encoding/compression pipelines
When Not To Use: When working with small fixed-size data in memory
Anti-Pattern: Reading entire file into memory, compressing, then encrypting in separate steps

Description: Define package-level error variables for well-known error conditions
When To Use: For errors that callers need to check with errors.Is; for domain boundaries
When Not To Use: When error needs dynamic context; when error is never checked by callers
Anti-Pattern: if err.Error() == "not found" { ... } // String comparison is fragile

Description: A goroutine that produces a sequence of values on a channel
When To Use: When generating a lazy sequence; when producer and consumer run at different speeds
When Not To Use: When all data fits in memory and a slice is simpler
Anti-Pattern: Generating entire sequence into a slice upfront: fibs := make([]int, 0)\nfor i := 0; i < 1000000; i++ { fibs = append(fibs, fib(i)) }

Description: Distribute work across multiple goroutines (fan-out) and collect results (fan-in)
When To Use: When tasks are independent and can run in parallel; when I/O bound work needs parallelism
When Not To Use: When tasks are dependent on each other; when overhead exceeds benefit
Anti-Pattern: Processing all items sequentially when they are independent

Description: Fixed number of goroutines processing jobs from a shared channel
When To Use: When you need bounded concurrency; when spawning unlimited goroutines would overwhelm resources
When Not To Use: When tasks are fast enough to run sequentially; when you need exactly one worker
Anti-Pattern: for _, job := range jobs { go process(job) } // Unbounded goroutines

Description: Prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to a failing service
When To Use: When calling external services that may fail; for API calls with known failure modes
When Not To Use: For local operations that rarely fail; when retry is handled at a different layer
Anti-Pattern: Retrying failed external calls indefinitely without any backoff or circuit breaking

Description: Control the rate of operations using a token bucket algorithm
When To Use: When calling rate-limited APIs; for protecting services from overload
When Not To Use: When traffic is already bounded; when rate limiting is handled by infrastructure
Anti-Pattern: time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond) // Fixed sleep instead of proper rate limiting

Description: Use a buffered channel as a counting semaphore to limit concurrent access
When To Use: When you need to limit concurrent access to a resource; for bounded parallelism
When Not To Use: When a simple mutex suffices; when using a proper worker pool instead
Anti-Pattern: for _, url := range urls { go fetch(url) } // No concurrency limit

Description: Implement publish-subscribe messaging pattern using Go channels
When To Use: When multiple consumers need to receive the same events; for decoupled event systems
When Not To Use: When you need persistent message queues; when ordering guarantees are critical
Anti-Pattern: Using shared mutable state with callbacks instead of channels

Description: Define roles with hierarchy (user < admin < superadmin) and check permissions in middleware. Use RequireRole(minRole) factory that creates middleware checking role level from JWT claims.
When To Use: When you need fine-grained access control; when roles group related permissions; for multi-tenant or admin systems
When Not To Use: For simple binary auth (authenticated/not); for public APIs without user roles
